sections.heading: What is Android?
sections.content: Android is an open-source, Linux-based mobile operating system developed by Google. It powers billions of smartphones, tablets, smartwatches, TVs, and cars worldwide. Android apps are primarily built using Kotlin (or Java) and run on the Android Runtime (ART).

sections.heading: Why Learn Android Development?
items: Largest mobile OS market share globally (70%+)
Huge job market and freelance opportunities
Backed by Google with regular updates
Rich ecosystem: Jetpack, Material Design, Firebase
Supports both Kotlin and Java
Publish apps to 2.5+ billion active devices

sections.heading: History of Android
sections.content: Android was founded in 2003 by Andy Rubin and acquired by Google in 2005. The first commercial Android device (HTC Dream) was released in 2008. Since then, Android has evolved through versions named after desserts (Cupcake, Donut, Oreo, Pie) and now follows numeric versioning (Android 12, 13, 14, 15).

sections.heading: Core Components of an Android App
items: Activity – A single screen with a user interface
Fragment – A reusable portion of UI within an Activity
Service – Runs background tasks without a UI
Broadcast Receiver – Listens for system-wide events (e.g., battery low)
Content Provider – Manages shared app data (e.g., contacts)
Intent – Messaging object used to communicate between components

rows.purpose: All your Kotlin/Java source code files
XML files defining the UI screens

sections.heading: Tools You Will Use
items: Android Studio – Official IDE for Android development
Android Emulator – Virtual device to test your apps
Gradle – Build system for Android projects
ADB (Android Debug Bridge) – Command-line tool for device communication
Logcat – Real-time log viewer for debugging
Firebase – Google's backend platform for Android apps

sections.heading: Android Development: Traditional XML vs Jetpack Compose
What You Will Build in This Course
items: Your first Hello World Android App
Multi-screen apps using Activities & Fragments
Apps that fetch data from the internet using Retrofit
Local database apps using Room
Modern UIs using Jetpack Compose
Production-ready apps following MVVM architecture

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.content: Before starting Android development, you should have a basic understanding of Kotlin programming (variables, functions, classes, and null safety). Familiarity with OOP concepts like inheritance and interfaces is also helpful. No prior Android experience is required — this course starts from the ground up.
